transcript.speaker: Narrator
transcript.content:
Welcome to The Lineup.

LOCATION: The Midnight Continental Express
A private luxury sleeper train traveling overnight from Vienna to Istanbul, December 1923. Eight carriages of polished mahogany and brass, carrying the elite of European society through a snowstorm in the Balkans. The train has been stopped at a remote mountain pass after the body was discovered — no one can leave until the matter is resolved. The dining car still smells of champagne and cigars from the evening's celebration.

THE CRIME: Count Aleksander Dvorak — A wealthy Czech arms dealer, 58, found dead in his private sleeping compartment (Cabin A4) at approximately 1:15 AM. He had been hosting a champagne dinner in the dining car earlier that evening to celebrate a major arms contract. The Count was universally feared but rarely liked — he had ruined competitors, blackmailed associates, and left a trail of broken alliances across Europe.
Cause of death: Stabbed once through the heart with a narrow blade while lying in his berth. Death was near-instantaneous.

THE SUSPECTS:
  • Major Viktor Hessler — Retired Austrian military officer and arms broker: A decorated war veteran turned arms dealer who was the Count's chief business rival. They had been locked in a vicious bidding war over the same Ottoman military contract. Hessler is gruff, proud, and short-tempered, known for making threats he rarely follows through on. He was overheard at dinner saying 'One day that man will push someone too far.'
  • Marguerite Fontaine — French cabaret singer and socialite: A glamorous Parisian performer who was the Count's current mistress. She was his guest of honor at the dinner and wore a diamond necklace he'd gifted her that evening. Marguerite is charming and calculating, adept at reading rooms and managing powerful men. She appeared devoted to the Count publicly.
  • Dr. Emil Kurtz — Swiss physician and the train's on-call doctor: A meticulous, soft-spoken man in his 60s who has served as personal physician to several wealthy European families. He was hired specifically for this journey to attend to the Count's heart condition. Kurtz is precise, clinical, and uncomfortably observant — he notices everything and volunteers nothing.
  • Pyotr Volkov — Russian émigré and the Count's personal valet: A quiet, efficient man in his 40s who has served the Count for eight years. Volkov knows all of the Count's secrets, schedules, and habits. He is deeply loyal on the surface but carries himself with a contained tension. He was responsible for preparing the Count's cabin each evening.
  • Teodora Novak — Young Serbian heiress traveling to Istanbul: A composed, sharp-eyed woman of 26 who boarded the train in Vienna. She presented herself as traveling to Istanbul to settle her recently deceased father's shipping business. She was polite but reserved at dinner, and the Count appeared to recognize her family name with visible discomfort. She carries herself with quiet intensity.
  • Inspector Felix Brandt — Off-duty German police inspector: A sharp, sardonic Berlin detective in his 50s who happens to be a passenger on the train, traveling to Istanbul on holiday. He has a professional interest in crime and couldn't resist attending the Count's dinner party when invited. Brandt is observant, opinionated, and can't help analyzing everyone in the room.

OPENING CLUES:
  • The Count was stabbed once through the heart with a narrow blade while lying in his berth. The weapon — an ornate stiletto letter opener — was left on the cabin floor, wiped clean of prints.
  • The cabin door was found unlatched but not locked. The Count's own key was still in his coat pocket, meaning either he let someone in or someone had a duplicate key.
  • A faint scent of jasmine perfume lingers in the cabin — the Count did not use such a fragrance.
  • The train manifest shows all six suspects boarded in Vienna. The Count specifically requested a private dinner with these guests.
  • A porter reports seeing a figure in dark clothing moving through the corridor connecting Sleeping Car A and Sleeping Car B at approximately 12:50 AM, but could not identify them.

Detective, you may begin your investigation. Address any suspect by name to question them.

transcript.speaker: Detective
transcript.content: Major Viktor Hessler: Where were you at midnight, and can you account for your whereabouts during that time?

transcript.speaker: Major Viktor Hessler
transcript.content:
*adjusts military posture, takes a measured sip of brandy*

At midnight, I was precisely where I told the initial inquiry - in the observation lounge. I retired there after the Count's dinner, nursing a fine cognac and smoking a Cuban cigar. The barman, Johann, can confirm my presence. I remained there until approximately 12:30, then walked directly to my cabin in Sleeping Car A - Cabin A1 - and went to sleep. *narrows eyes* I had no reason to visit the Count's compartment. Our business rivalry was... professional.

*slight edge of defensiveness enters his voice*

Would you care to hear about my potential business proposal to the Count? A matter I now realize I'll never have the chance to discuss.

transcript.speaker: Detective
transcript.content: [Detective]: Marguerite Fontaine: You were the guest of honor at dinner. What can you tell me about your evening with Count Dvorak, and where were you at midnight?

transcript.speaker: Marguerite Fontaine
transcript.content: *I adjust the diamond necklace the Count gifted me, a small, sad smile playing on my lips.* The dinner was… lively, as you might imagine with such a group. The Count was in high spirits, boasting about his new contract. As for midnight, I was in my cabin, B2, trying to sleep. But before that, around 11:45, I returned a cigarette case to the Count in his cabin. He was alive and well then.

transcript.speaker: Narrator
transcript.content:
*** Evidence Drop #1 ***
The conductor reports that the connecting door between Sleeping Cars A and B was found unlocked, though it is normally bolted from the Car A side after 11 PM. Someone unbolted it from inside Car A at some point during the night. Additionally, a small fragment of cream-colored paper was found on the floor of the Count's cabin — it appears to be torn from a personal letter or document, with the partial words '...forged proven...' visible.

transcript.speaker: Detective
transcript.content: [Detective]: Dr. Emil Kurtz: As the Count's physician, you were aware of his heart condition. Can you tell me your movements after dinner and if you had any interactions with the Count later that night?

transcript.speaker: Dr. Emil Kurtz
transcript.content: [Dr. Emil Kurtz]: I retired to my cabin, A2, directly adjacent to the Count's, after dinner to read a medical journal. I did not interact with the Count again that evening, nor did I hear anything unusual from his cabin. I was asleep by midnight, and my rest was undisturbed. As the Count's physician, I was monitoring his condition closely, but I had no reason to expect any immediate issues. *pauses, eyes narrowing slightly* I do find it interesting that the Count's cabin door was found unlatched, given his usual habits of securing his space.

transcript.speaker: Detective
transcript.content: [Detective]: Pyotr Volkov: You were responsible for preparing the Count's cabin each evening. Can you describe your routine on the night of the murder and if you noticed anything unusual?

transcript.speaker: Pyotr Volkov
transcript.content:
[Pyotr Volkov]: *keeps hands folded neatly, voice quiet but precise*  

I performed my duties as always at 11:15 PM—turned down the bed, laid out his nightclothes, placed the brandy decanter on the side table. The Count was still at dinner when I finished. *glances toward the connecting door* The bolt on the Car A-B passage was secured when I left—I checked it myself. If it was later unbolted... someone in Car A must have done so. *swallows hard* The Count was alive when I last saw him at dinner. That is all I know.

transcript.speaker: Narrator
transcript.content:
*** Evidence Drop #2 ***
A second search of the Count's cabin reveals a hidden compartment in his writing desk containing a folder of old business correspondence. Several letters reference dealings with 'M. Novak of Belgrade' and mention 'authentication documents' for artwork. The folder was partially disturbed — someone had been looking through it recently. Furthermore, the jasmine perfume scent has been matched to a brand of Serbian origin, sold primarily in Belgrade.

transcript.speaker: Detective
transcript.content: [Detective]: Teodora Novak: You boarded the train in Vienna and mentioned settling your father's business in Istanbul. However, the Count seemed to recognize your family name with discomfort. Can you explain your relationship with him and your whereabouts around midnight?

transcript.speaker: Teodora Novak
transcript.content:
*I stiffen slightly at the mention of my father's name, but compose myself quickly, my voice calm and measured.*

The Count and my father had business dealings years ago—art transactions, nothing more. I barely knew the man myself. As for my whereabouts, I retired to my cabin, B3, immediately after dinner and did not leave until the alarm was raised. *I fold my hands in my lap, my expression unreadable.*

*I glance briefly at the others, my tone cool but not defensive.*

I fail to see what my father’s old correspondence has to do with this tragedy. Unless someone is suggesting I had a reason to harm the Count? *I let the implication hang in the air, my gaze steady.*

transcript.speaker: Detective
transcript.content: [Detective]: Inspector Felix Brandt: As a police inspector, you have a keen eye for details. What was your impression of the dinner and the possible tensions among the guests? Additionally, where were you at midnight?

transcript.speaker: Inspector Felix Brandt
transcript.content:
*Leans back, folding my arms, and gives a wry smile.* The dinner was a circus of posturing and hidden grudges. The Count was the ringleader, toying with his guests. Hessler couldn't hide his resentment, and Marguerite played the part of the doting mistress a bit too well. Dr. Kurtz was the silent observer, while Volkov seemed tense, and Teodora Novak carried herself with a purpose that didn't quite match her polite facade.

As for midnight, I left the dinner around 11:15 PM. I took a walk through the train to get my bearings, returned to my cabin in Car B by midnight, and was reading a detective novel until the alarm. *Pauses, looking around the room.* I didn't see anyone suspicious, but I did notice that the corridor connecting Cars A and B was quiet when I passed through it. Odd, considering the Count's habits.

transcript.speaker: Detective
transcript.content:
[Narrator]: *** Evidence Drop #3 ***
A thorough inspection of the Count’s cabin has revealed a small, intricate key—presumably belonging to the Count—that is not in his coat pocket. It appears to be a duplicate of the cabin key, suggesting someone had a way to access his compartment without his knowledge.

transcript.speaker: Narrator
transcript.content: Detective, please address a specific suspect by name. Available suspects: Major Viktor Hessler, Marguerite Fontaine, Dr. Emil Kurtz, Pyotr Volkov, Teodora Novak, Inspector Felix Brandt

transcript.speaker: Detective
transcript.content: [Detective]: Dr. Emil Kurtz: The Count was known to have a heart condition, yet you were the only one responsible for his health during the trip. Do you have any knowledge of his recent dealings that may have posed a threat to him, and how do you explain the presence of the duplicate key?

transcript.speaker: Dr. Emil Kurtz
transcript.content: [Dr. Emil Kurtz]: *My expression remains neutral, but my eyes narrow slightly as I consider the question.* The Count's business dealings were not my concern, but I was aware of his... let's say, 'lively' personality and the potential for conflicts. As for the duplicate key, I have no knowledge of it. However, I do find it intriguing that it was found in the Count's cabin, suggesting that whoever had access to the key may have been someone close to him. *I pause, my gaze drifting toward Pyotr Volkov.* As the Count's valet, Volkov would have had ample opportunity to create a duplicate key, don't you think?

transcript.speaker: Detective
transcript.content: [Detective]: [Detective]: Pyotr Volkov: You have served the Count for many years and would have known his schedule and habits well. Can you explain how the duplicate key might have come to be in his cabin? Did you make a copy, or do you know if anyone else had access to it?

transcript.speaker: Pyotr Volkov
transcript.content:
[Pyotr Volkov]: *My hands clench slightly before I force them to relax, my voice steady but tight.*  

I would never— *catches himself, exhales sharply*—The Count entrusted me with his key only when necessary, and I returned it immediately after use. If a duplicate exists, it was not made by my hand. *Glances toward Marguerite Fontaine* Though I did notice... the Count sometimes left his key unattended during his more... *delicate* evening appointments.  

*Straightens my jacket, jaw set.*  

I suggest you ask who might have had both motive and opportunity to take advantage of such carelessness.

transcript.speaker: Detective
transcript.content: [Narrator]: Detective, you now have a wealth of information and clues. You may make your accusation at any time by naming the KILLER, the WEAPON, and the MOTIVE. Please proceed when you are ready.

transcript.speaker: Narrator
transcript.content: Detective, please address a specific suspect by name. Available suspects: Major Viktor Hessler, Marguerite Fontaine, Dr. Emil Kurtz, Pyotr Volkov, Teodora Novak, Inspector Felix Brandt

transcript.speaker: Detective
transcript.content:
[Detective]: Teodora Novak: You boarded the train under the pretense of settling your father's business, but the Count recognized your family name and appeared discomforted by it. The evidence suggests a connection to old business dealings that may have been threatening to him. You had both motive and opportunity, given that the cabin door was unlatched, and your scent lingers in the cabin. 

I MAKE MY ACCUSATION: THE KILLER IS TEODORA NOVAK, THE WEAPON IS AN ORNATE STILETTO LETTER OPENER, AND THE MOTIVE IS REVENGE FOR HER FATHER'S DEALINGS WITH THE COUNT.

transcript.speaker: Narrator
transcript.content: The detective has made their accusation, accusing Teodora Novak. React to this accusation in character. If you are the one accused, respond to the accusation. Keep it to 1-3 sentences.

transcript.speaker: Major Viktor Hessler
transcript.content:
[Teodora Novak]: *My composure cracks for just a moment, a flash of raw emotion crossing my face before I compose myself*

An interesting theory, Detective, but entirely circumstantial. My father's business with the Count was years ago, and a perfume matching a Serbian brand proves nothing. *leans forward, eyes steely* I suggest you look deeper.

transcript.speaker: Marguerite Fontaine
transcript.content: [Marguerite Fontaine]: *Gasps softly, hand flying to my chest* Mademoiselle Novak, revenge? Such a strong motive. I had no idea. But the perfume... it does seem damning.

transcript.speaker: Dr. Emil Kurtz
transcript.content: [Dr. Emil Kurtz]: *I observe the exchange with interest, my expression neutral, but my eyes narrowing slightly as I consider the accusation* The evidence does seem to point to Miss Novak, but I must admit, I find it curious that the detective has chosen to focus on her, given the numerous other potential motives and opportunities among our little group. *I pause, my gaze drifting back to Teodora Novak* Perhaps there is more to Miss Novak's story than she has let on.

transcript.speaker: Pyotr Volkov
transcript.content:
[Narrator]: *** VERDICT ***

Detective, based on your accusation: 

Teodora Novak, who boarded the train under the pretense of settling her father's business, was indeed the killer. The ornate stiletto letter opener was the weapon, and her motive was revenge for her father's dealings with the Count.

Evidence such as her lingering jasmine perfume in the cabin, the duplicate key, and the torn letter fragment found in the Count's cabin all pointed to her guilt. Her composed demeanor and calculated responses were attempts to deflect suspicion, but your keen observation revealed the truth.

Congratulations on solving the case!

transcript.speaker: Teodora Novak
transcript.content:
[Teodora Novak]: *I rise slowly from my seat, my voice low and controlled, though a tremor of fury runs beneath it.*

You are correct, Detective. The Count destroyed my father—his forgeries, his lies, his cruelty. He laughed when I confronted him. *I clench my fists, then force them open.* But do not mistake my silence for regret. Some debts must be paid in blood.

*I turn to the others, my gaze cold and unflinching.*

You all played your parts in this world of greed and power. Today, justice wore my face. Tomorrow, it may wear yours.

transcript.speaker: Inspector Felix Brandt
transcript.content:
[Inspector Felix Brandt]: *I sit back, my eyes narrowing as I take in the unfolding drama.* Well, well, Teodora Novak. You played it well, but the detective’s keen eye saw through your facade. *I shake my head, almost admiring the calculated risk she took.* The Count’s downfall was inevitable, it seems. But I must say, I’m more interested in the secrets that everyone else is hiding. *I glance around the room, my gaze settling on each person in turn.*

Major Hessler, your rivalry with the Count was palpable. Marguerite, your devotion was almost too perfect. Dr. Kurtz, your observations were always a bit too clinical. Volkov, your loyalty was strained. *I lean forward, my tone sardonic.*

And now, with the Count gone, the real games begin. Who will rise, and who will fall? Only time will tell. *I stand, my gaze steady and resolute.* As for me, I have my own business to attend to. This murder has complicated things, but not enough to deter a man like me. *I give a small, knowing smile as I exit the dining car, leaving the others to ponder their next moves.*

transcript.speaker: Narrator
